title: Chapter 9: Sparks in the Dark
paragraphs: While Avotu and Alexander listened to the funeral knell of hopes in the castle of the fading king, the rest of the strangers' group tried to find themselves on the lower ashy levels of Agdash. The Mechanics' Guild was located in a massive, stiflingly-cramped semi-basement room, carved directly into the volcanic rock. Inside there was an unbearable, physically crushing sound on the eardrums: the hard clanking of metal on metal, the aggressive hissing of steam escaping under pressure, and the pulsing, unhealthy hum of overloaded magical crystals. The air in the workshop was saturated with a thick, pungent smell of hot machine oil and sharp ozone.

Ravan, gloomier than a storm cloud, immediately sat down in the darkest corner of the workshop, staring blankly, with a vacant look at the soot-stained wall. Even the hand brilliantly healed by the elf's magic didn't return his will to live. The former leader, used to physical dominance in his cozy earthly world, had proved to be a helpless grain of sand in the face of monsters tearing reality apart. He felt like a burden to his friends. Anar, whose face remained impassive, leaned against a stone doorpost, watching the bustle with interest but without obvious enthusiasm. Но для Жени это грязное, шумное место стало настоящим наркотиком.
The Master of the Guild—a scarred one-eyed dwarf named Brann, whose left arm from shoulder to fingertips was replaced by a massive, gear-driven, rough prosthetic—was loudly and dirtily swearing.

"Turn off the jet feed! The crystal is overheating to critical, the valve won't hold the pressure!" Brann yelled frantically, pounding with a huge wrench on a pot-bellied red boiler that was already white-hot and vibrating threateningly.

Several elven apprentices with faces twisted with terror waved their hands, trying with shaking fingers to weave a restraining spell, but their chaotic mana only accelerated the thermodynamic pressure inside the reservoir even more.

Zhenya, not understanding how himself, forgot about his human fear. His brain, which for decades had methodically disassembled the most complex internal combustion engines in some damp brick garage in his past life, instantly built a clear diagram in his head. Magic remained magic, but basic physics—is physics everywhere. Critical pressure seeks a physical exit.

The worker, without thinking twice, pushed aside the stunned elven mage, grabbed a heavy steel pipe lying on the workbench, and rigidly inserted it between the flywheel of the emergency manual reset and the stone wall. Leaning with all his human weight on the improvised lever, he turned the soured, rusty mechanism with a crunch.

A deafening, shrill whistle echoed. A thick, poisonous-violet cloud of magical steam with an animal roar escaped into the ceiling ventilation shaft, melting the grate. The boiler slowly, with a squeak, emitting a metallic moan, began to cool down. The metal stopped glowing crimson.

A dead, ringing silence fell in the workshop, interrupted only by the intermittent hissing of the vented steam. One-eyed Brann slowly, not believing his eyes, turned to the completely ordinary stranger in a dirty earthly jacket.

"You... you didn't use mana streams. You used the cursed lever and naked physical pull," the dwarf stated in a скрипучим voice full of awe. A sincere respect flared in his only healthy eye. "Who are you, boy from the dirt?"

"I'm Zhenya. And you have here..." he embarrassedly wiped his soot-stained hands on his pants, "your safety valve is radically wrongly designed by section. The reset chamber is too narrow for such a catastrophic volume of coolant."
Brann laughed, and this laugh was like the rumble of a mountain rockfall. A big, sincere laugh in a city that was supposed to die tomorrow.

"I like you, human! A stranger whose head isn't stuffed with useless elven tricks! This section of the smithy—is yours, Zhenya. Take any metal, take empty demonic crystals. Show the master what your strange, flat mind is capable of."

While Zhenya, wearing thick fireproof leather gloves, joyfully rattled heavy tools, Anar silently approached Ravan. The former dentist didn't even raise his head. His once mighty shoulders slumped under the invisible weight of absolute helplessness.

"We aren't going home, are we?" Ravan asked hollowly, in a cracked, old man's voice, looking into the void. "I'll never see Marina again... I'll never hear my Dimka waking up and laughing. We'll rot here, Anar. We are meat for their monsters."

"I don't know," Anar calmly sat down nearby, thoughtfully forming a small, safe blue spark between his relaxed fingers, watching its dance. "Maybe we will rot in the ash. Or maybe not. But sitting and pitying ourselves—is mathematically unprofitable and too energy-consuming. If these cursed portals open here—it means that by the laws of physics, they can open back. We just need to live long enough for that moment."
Ravan heavily raised his red, sleep-deprived eyes to Anar.

"Live long enough..." he whispered, clenching his healed fist with such uncontrollable force that the knuckles turned white. "In this world, a piece of meat isn't enough to survive. I am nobody without my power. I cannot protect you."

At that moment, the soot-smeared Zhenya confidently approached his sad friends. With a heavy, dull thud, he lowered a massive, roughly welded structure from thick sheets of metal onto the table in front of Ravan. It was a steel gauntlet extending to the elbow, thickly studded with valves and reinforced tubes. In its center, on the outside of the palm, an shard of a red demonic energy crystal pulsed dimly and sinisterly.

"Remember you whined about a piece of meat, Rav?" Zhenya smiled warmly, brotherly, wiping the sweat from his forehead. "Meat is really enough. If you add turbocharging and a bit of our pneumatics to the meat."
